words.definition: the quality of having more than one possible meaning
example_sentences: The ambiguity of the law caused confusion.
Her statement was full of ambiguity — nobody knew what she meant.
Ambiguity in a contract can lead to disputes.
words.pronunciation_tip: am-bih-GYOO-ih-tee — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'it has two meanings' — say 'it is ambiguous' or 'there is ambiguity'.

words.definition: the freedom to govern yourself or make your own decisions
example_sentences: She values her autonomy and doesn't like being micromanaged.
The region fought for greater autonomy from the central government.
Employees perform better when given autonomy.
words.pronunciation_tip: aw-TON-uh-mee — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'freedom to decide' as a noun — say 'autonomy'.

words.definition: the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
example_sentences: She is a person of absolute integrity.
Integrity is the most important quality in a leader.
He acted with complete integrity throughout the process.
words.pronunciation_tip: in-TEG-rih-tee — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he is honest' as a noun — say 'he has integrity'.

words.definition: the way you understand or interpret something
example_sentences: Her perception of the situation was completely wrong.
Perception is often more important than reality in business.
Public perception of the company has improved.
words.pronunciation_tip: per-SEP-shun — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'what she thinks' always — say 'her perception of'.

words.definition: a typical example or model; a framework for thinking
example_sentences: The internet created a new paradigm for communication.
Her research challenged the existing scientific paradigm.
A paradigm shift happens when old ideas are replaced.
words.pronunciation_tip: PAIR-uh-dym — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a new way of thinking' every time — say 'a paradigm' or 'a paradigm shift'.

words.definition: the ability to recover quickly from difficulties
example_sentences: Children who develop resilience cope better with setbacks.
Economic resilience helped the country recover quickly.
Resilience is built through facing and overcoming challenges.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-ZIL-ee-unss — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she recovers quickly from problems' as a noun — say 'she has resilience'.

words.definition: the fact of being responsible and expected to explain your actions
example_sentences: Accountability is essential in public office.
There must be greater accountability in the banking sector.
She holds her team to a high standard of accountability.
words.pronunciation_tip: uh-kown-tuh-BIL-ih-tee — six syllables, stress on fourth.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'responsibility to explain' as a noun — say 'accountability'.

words.definition: a particular way of thinking about something based on experience
example_sentences: Try to see it from her perspective.
A different perspective can lead to a better solution.
Travel broadens your perspective.
words.pronunciation_tip: per-SPEK-tiv — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'her point of view' every time — say 'her perspective'.
words.collocations: ['from a different perspective', 'broaden your perspective', 'change perspective']

words.definition: a situation where two things cannot both be true at the same time
example_sentences: His actions were in direct contradiction to his words.
There are several contradictions in the report.
The contradiction between freedom and security is ancient.
words.pronunciation_tip: kon-truh-DIK-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'two things that are opposite and can't be true' — say 'a contradiction'.

words.definition: a general agreement among a group of people
example_sentences: There is no consensus on the best way forward.
The committee reached consensus after three hours of discussion.
The scientific consensus on climate change is clear.
words.pronunciation_tip: kun-SEN-sus — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'everyone agrees' as a noun — say 'there is consensus'.

words.definition: openness and honesty so people can see what is happening
example_sentences: The public demands greater transparency from government.
She manages her team with complete transparency.
Financial transparency is required by law.
words.pronunciation_tip: trans-PAIR-un-see — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'being open and honest' as a noun — say 'transparency'.

words.definition: a set of beliefs or principles held by a group or organisation
example_sentences: The two parties are driven by very different ideologies.
Religious ideology played a major role in the conflict.
She questioned the underlying ideology of the movement.
words.pronunciation_tip: eye-dee-OL-uh-jee — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'set of beliefs' every time — say 'ideology'.

words.definition: an unfair preference for or against something or someone
example_sentences: The study's results show a clear bias towards younger participants.
She tried to remove any personal bias from her research.
Unconscious bias affects decisions even when we don't realise it.
words.pronunciation_tip: BY-us — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'unfair preference' every time — say 'bias'.

words.definition: a fixed oversimplified idea about a type of person or thing
example_sentences: She challenged the stereotype that women aren't good at maths.
Stereotypes can be harmful even when well-intentioned.
Not all stereotypes are negative but they are always reductive.
words.pronunciation_tip: STEER-ee-uh-typ — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a fixed idea about a group' every time — say 'a stereotype'.

words.definition: a situation that seems self-contradictory but may be true
example_sentences: It's a paradox — working less can actually increase productivity.
The freedom paradox: too many choices lead to less happiness.
She found the paradox of fame difficult to deal with.
words.pronunciation_tip: PAIR-uh-doks — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a strange contradiction that is actually true' — say 'a paradox'.

words.definition: a subtle difference in meaning, feeling, or understanding
example_sentences: The translation lost the nuance of the original poem.
She handled the situation with great nuance.
Political issues rarely have simple answers — nuance is essential.
words.pronunciation_tip: NYOO-anss — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a small difference' always — say 'nuance' or 'nuanced approach'.

words.definition: an earlier event or decision that guides future decisions
example_sentences: The ruling set a precedent for all future cases.
There is no precedent for this kind of situation.
She was reluctant to set a precedent.
words.pronunciation_tip: PRES-ih-dunt — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'example that guides future decisions' every time — say 'precedent'.

words.definition: a possible consequence or conclusion suggested by something
example_sentences: The implications of the decision are serious.
She didn't fully consider the implications.
What are the implications for small businesses?
words.pronunciation_tip: im-plih-KAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the result that might happen' — say 'the implication' or 'the implications'.

words.definition: a situation in which a difficult choice must be made
example_sentences: She faced a dilemma: stay loyal or tell the truth.
The ethical dilemma had no easy answer.
The doctor was caught in a moral dilemma.
words.pronunciation_tip: dih-LEM-uh — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a difficult choice' every time — say 'a dilemma'.

words.definition: the set of reasons behind a decision or belief
example_sentences: She explained the rationale for her decision.
The report outlined the rationale behind the new policy.
Without a clear rationale, the decision seemed arbitrary.
words.pronunciation_tip: rash-uh-NAL — three syllables, stress on last.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the reason for the decision' every time — say 'the rationale'.
